claim: Low-density ferritic steels with Al contents of 5, 6, 6.8, and 9 wt% were prepared using vacuum arc melting of IF steel and pure aluminium, with multiple remelting to reduce segregation.
evidence_text: The charges consisting of interstitial free (IF) steel and pure aluminium were melted in a vacuum arc furnace to prepare low-density steels with aluminium contents of 5, 6, 6.8 and 9 wt%. The obtained circular buttons of diameter 10 cm and height 5 cm were re-melted multiple times (7–8 times) to remove segregation.

claim: The Fe-6.8Al alloy was cold rolled to 98% reduction in thickness and then annealed at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min.
evidence_text: The Fe-6.8Al alloy was cold rolled (CR) to 98% reduction in thickness and annealed at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min.

claim: Powder samples for Mössbauer spectroscopy and SAXS were prepared by filing, sealed under high vacuum of 10^-5 mbar, and annealed at 600 °C for 60 min to relieve internal stress.
evidence_text: The powder samples were then wrapped in a copper foil and sealed in a glass tube under high vacuum (10^{-5} mBar) and annealing was performed at 600 °C for 60 min. The samples were annealed to remove the internal stress generated during the creation of powder particles by filing.

claim: X-ray diffraction was performed using Cu-Kα radiation, scanning from 40° to 120° 2θ with a step size of 0.03°, and the (110) peak was used for dislocation density analysis via the Variance method.
evidence_text: X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using X-Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. All the samples were scanned from an angle of 40° to 120° with a step size of 0.03°. For the determination of dislocation density, only the (110) XRD peak profile was used in the present study. The dislocation density was determined using the Variance method developed by Wilson and Groma [20–23].

claim: SAXS measurements utilized a laboratory dual-energy Mo-Cr system with camera lengths of 550 and 2400 mm, covering a q-range from 0.0254 to 10 nm⁻¹.
evidence_text: SAXS measurements were carried out by using a laboratory-based dual energy Mo-Cr-SAXS system (Xeuss, Xenocs, France) [16]. Use of dual X-ray energies and different camera lengths of 550 and 2400 mm allows covering of a wide q-range from 0.0254 to 10 nm⁻¹, where q (= 4π/λ sinθ) is the modulus of momentum transfer.

claim: Mössbauer spectroscopy was performed at room temperature in transmission geometry with a 57Co(Rh) source and velocity calibrated using a pure α-Fe foil.
evidence_text: Mössbauer spectroscopy was carried out at room temperature in transmission geometry by using a conventional constant acceleration Doppler velocity drive with 57Co (Rh-matrix) source. The velocity of the drive was calibrated by a pure iron (α-Fe) foil.

claim: Uniaxial tensile tests were performed on sub-size specimens with a gauge length of 6 mm and width of 2 mm at a strain rate of 10⁻³ s⁻¹, with the gauge section parallel to the rolling direction.
evidence_text: Sub size tensile specimens were prepared with a gauge length of 6 mm and width of 2 mm as reported previously [15]. The gauge section was machined parallel to rolling direction (RD) of the rolled sheet. Three tensile tests were conducted for each sample condition. Uniaxial tensile tests were performed at a strain rate of 10^{-3} s^{-1} in an Instron 5967 screw driven tensile testing machine.

claim: The Fe-6.8Al alloy was cold rolled to 98% reduction in thickness and annealed at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min.
evidence_text: The Fe-6.8Al alloy was cold rolled (CR) to 98% reduction in thickness and annealed at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min.

claim: The dislocation density of the Fe-6.8Al samples was determined from the (110) XRD peak profile using the Variance method, with diffraction scans from 40° to 120° 2θ at a step of 0.03° using Cu-Kα radiation.
evidence_text: All the samples were scanned from an angle of 40° to 120° with a step size of 0.03°. For the determination of dislocation density, only the (110) XRD peak profile was used in the present study. The dislocation density was determined using the Variance method developed by Wilson and Groma [20–23].

claim: Tensile specimens with a gauge length of 6 mm and width of 2 mm were machined parallel to the rolling direction and uniaxially tested at a strain rate of 10⁻³ s⁻¹, with three specimens per condition.
evidence_text: Sub size tensile specimens were prepared with a gauge length of 6 mm and width of 2 mm as reported previously [15]. Uniaxial tensile tests were performed at a strain rate of 10⁻³ s⁻¹ in an Instron 5967 screw driven tensile testing machine. Three tensile tests were conducted for each sample condition.

claim: Mössbauer spectra were fitted with a distribution of up to 40 sextets using the NORMOS program, and the hyperfine field B was described by B = B0 - n1ΔB1 - n2ΔB2 with B0=33 T, ΔB1=2.4 T, ΔB2=0.5 T.
evidence_text: the experimentally measured Mössbauer spectrum of each Fe-Al composition was fitted by the NORMOS program to obtain the probability distribution P(Bhf) ... The Bhf distribution consists of up to 40 sextets. B = B0 - n1 ΔB1 - n2 ΔB2 (1) where B0 = 33 T, ΔB1 = 2.4 T and ΔB2 = 0.5 T.

claim: The Mössbauer spectrum of α-Fe foil shows a hyperfine field peak at 33 T, corresponding to a local atomic configuration of (8Fe)nn + (6Fe)nnn.
evidence_text: Fig. 1a ... The corresponding Bhf distribution is presented in Fig. 1b shows peaks at 33 T and ~20 T for Fe foil. This hyperfine field of 33 T corresponds to a local atomic arrangement of eight Fe atoms as first nearest neighbour (nn) and six Fe atoms as second nearest neighbour (nnn).

claim: All studied Fe-Al low-density steels (5, 6, 6.8, 9 wt% Al) exhibit a peak at Bhf ≈ 30.6 T, which can be attributed to either (7Fe+1Al)nn+(6Fe)nnn solid solution configuration or (8Fe)nn+(6Al)nnn characteristic of Fe3Al ordered phase.
evidence_text: We have observed a peak at Bhf = 30.6 T in all the studied Fe-Al low-density steel samples. Although the 30.6 T component may be associated with the local atomic configuration of (7Fe + 1Al)nn + (6Fe)nnn, the presence of Fe3Al phase corresponding to the atomic configuration of (8Fe)nn + (6Al)nnn cannot be ruled out due to the close proximity of the peaks corresponding to the two different configurations.

claim: Low-intensity peaks at Bhf ~23 T are observed in Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al and Fe-9Al, indicating the possible presence of Fe3Al phase with a low volume fraction, while this peak is absent in Fe-5Al.
evidence_text: For the samples with Al content of 6, 6.8 and 9, we have observed low-intensity peaks at ~23 T. This may also be a possible indicator for the presence of Fe3Al phase with a low volume fraction. The absence of peak at 23 T for the Fe-5Al sample may be due to its low volume fraction.

claim: For Fe-Al alloys with Al > 24 wt%, the Mössbauer hyperfine fields for DO3 (Fe3Al) and B2 (FeAl) phases are reported as 30 T and 21 T, respectively.
evidence_text: The calculated hyperfine field values for the Mössbauer spectra of DO3 (Fe3Al) and B2 (FeAl) phases in Fe-Al alloys (with Al > 24 wt%) are reported to be 30 T and 21 T respectively [24,25].

claim: A hyperfine field peak at 30.6 T observed in all Fe-Al low-density steel samples may correspond to either a local atomic configuration of (7Fe+1Al)nn+(6Fe)nnn or the Fe3Al phase with (8Fe)nn+(6Al)nnn, because the two configurations produce nearly identical hyperfine fields.
evidence_text: We have observed a peak at B_hf = 30.6 T in all the studied Fe-Al low-density steel samples. Although the 30.6 T component may be associated with the local atomic configuration of (7Fe + 1Al)nn + (6Fe)nnn, the presence of Fe3Al phase corresponding to the atomic configuration of (8Fe)nn + (6Al)nnn cannot be ruled out due to the close proximity of the peaks.

claim: Low-intensity peaks at approximately 23 T in the Mössbauer spectra of Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al and Fe-9Al alloys suggest the presence of Fe3Al phase with a low volume fraction, while their absence in Fe-5Al is attributed to an even lower volume fraction.
evidence_text: For the samples with Al content of 6, 6.8 and 9, we have observed low-intensity peaks at ~23 T. This may also be a possible indicator for the presence of Fe3Al phase with a low volume fraction. The absence of peak at 23 T for the Fe-5Al sample may be due to its low volume fraction.

claim: A weak scattering peak between q = 0.3 and 3 nm^{-1} in the SAXS intensity profiles originates from nano-sized ordered phase, and the scattering intensity increases with aluminium content, accompanied by a shift of Guinier regions to lower q, confirming an increase in ordered phase size.
evidence_text: The scattering from the nano-sized ordered phase can clearly be observed as a weak peak between q = 0.3 – 3 nm^{-1}. It can be observed from Fig. 3 that the scattering intensity is increasing with Al-content, implying the increase of nano-sized ordered phase and the Guinier regions are shifting towards the low q-region confirming the increase in sizes of the ordered phase.

claim: In the SAXS profiles, the intensity at q < 0.3 nm^{-1} decays as q^{-4}, indicating scattering from larger heterogeneities such as grains and grain boundaries.
evidence_text: at the low qregion (below 0.3 nm−1), the intensity decays as a function of q−4 with q, indicating that this intensity originates from larger heterogeneities that are present in the alloy. The large heterogeneities may be grains and/or grain boundaries of the alloys.

claim: SAXS model fitting (Eq. 3) shows that the average diameter of the Fe3Al ordered phase increases from 2.5±0.15 nm for Fe-5Al to 3.75±0.2 nm for Fe-9Al, and the volume fraction rises with aluminium content due to a greater driving force for ordering.
evidence_text: The diameter of Fe3Al ordered phase increased from 2.5 ± 0.15 nm to 3.75 ± 0.2 nm with increasing Al content. The increase in volume fraction and size of the ordered phase could be due to the increasing driving force for the order phase formation with increasing Al addition (wt%).

claim: The volume fractions of the ordered phase determined by SAXS are systematically lower than those obtained by Mössbauer spectroscopy because SAXS is sensitive only to ordered regions with sharp boundaries, whereas Mössbauer spectroscopy detects all local atomic environments including diffuse boundaries.
evidence_text: It is to be noted that the volume fractions (Fig. 4) of the ordered phase observed by SAXS are lower than that of the DO3 phase observed by Mössbauer spectroscopy. This is due to the fact that SAXS provides collective information about the ordered phase having a sharper boundary whereas Mössbauer spectroscopy traces all local environments, including those having diffused boundaries.

claim: The 98% cold rolled Fe-6.8Al sample contains heavily elongated, pancaked grains with shear bands oriented at approximately 40° to the rolling direction; the average width of the deformed lamellae is 2.6 ± 1.2 µm.
evidence_text: The cold rolled sample ... shows heavily elongated and pancaked grains along the rolling directions. The presence of shear bands can be observed indicating non-uniform deformation. Shear bands were observed at an angle of ~40° with the RD. The average width of the deformed lamellae is 2.6 ± 1.2 µm.

claim: After annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, the Fe-6.8Al alloy exhibits equiaxed fully recrystallised grains with a grain size of 25.2 ± 5.0 µm (SEM); extending the annealing to 60 min leads to grain growth with a grain size of 48.3 ± 1.2 µm (SEM) (Table 2).
evidence_text: The sample annealed for 5 min ... and 60 min (Fig. 5) shows equiaxed and fully recrystallised grains. The grain/crystallite size has been reported in Table 2. Grain growth can be observed for the sample annealed for 60 min as compared to 5 min due to increase in grain size (25.2–48.3 µm).

claim: The crystallite size of the 98% cold rolled Fe-6.8Al sample determined by XRD line profile analysis is 102.3 ± 9.8 nm (Table 2).
evidence_text: 98% CR 102.3 ± 9.8 XRD analysis

claim: Dark-field TEM imaging of Fe-6.8Al shows a very fine ordered phase (bright contrast) with a measured size of 2.1 ± 0.2 nm; the corresponding diffraction pattern contains strong matrix spots and weak superlattice reflections confirming the presence of ordered phase, and the size agrees with SAXS results.
evidence_text: The dark field image shows the presence of very fine ordered phase (bright) which are seen to be associated with dark background. The size of the ordered phase has been determined to be 2.1 ± 0.2 nm. The size of the ordered phase measured from TEM is in correlation to that determined from SAXS studies (cf. Fig. 4).

claim: The ordered phase in Fe-6.8Al samples, measured by TEM, has a size of 2.1±0.2 nm, which is consistent with SAXS results (cf. Fig. 4).
evidence_text: The size of the ordered phase has been determined to be $2.1 \pm 0.2$ nm. The size of the ordered phase measured from TEM is in correlation to that determined from SAXS studies (cf. Fig. 4).

claim: Lattice micro-strain in Fe-6.8Al decreases from 1.46×10⁻³ in the 98% cold rolled condition to 9.46×10⁻⁴ after annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, and further to 1.46×10⁻⁴ after 60 min, due to recrystallization.
evidence_text: It can be observed that the lattice micro-strain decreases after annealing from the cold rolled state which is due to recrystallization of the samples (Table 3). The decrease is sharp from the 98%CR sample to $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ 5 min annealed sample whereas it is shallow from 5 min to 60 min annealed sample as recrystallization is already completed upon annealing at for 5 min [15].

claim: The yield strength of Fe-6.8Al is 1800 MPa after 98% cold rolling, 700 MPa after annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, and 265 MPa after 60 min; corresponding uniform elongations are 1.2%, 20.0%, and 29.8%, and yield ratios are 0.85 (5 min) and 0.67 (60 min).
evidence_text: The yield strength of the 98% CR sample (= 1800 MPa) is much higher than for the sample annealed at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 60 min $( = 2 6 5 \mathrm { M P a } )$ (Fig. 8a, Table 4).

claim: The yield strength of 1800 MPa in 98%CR Fe-6.8Al correlates with a dislocation density of ~2.2×10¹⁵ m⁻², while annealing at 750 °C for 60 min reduces dislocation density to ~10¹² m⁻², leading to a drop in yield strength to 265 MPa, identifying dislocation strengthening as a main mechanism.
evidence_text: The drastic change in yield strength (1800 MPa, 98% CR to $265 \mathrm{MPa}$ , annealed at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , 60 min) upon the change in dislocation density from $\sim 2.2 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ (98%CR) to $\sim 10^{12} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ (annealed at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , 60 min) identifies strengthening from dislocations to be one of the main strengthening mechanism.

claim: Solid solution strengthening (τ_ss) for Fe-6.8Al was calculated as 95.6 MPa using G = 82 GPa, solute concentration c = 6.8 wt%, and ε_s = 0.45.
evidence_text: The value of τ_ss was calculated as 95.6 MPa for the Fe-6.8Al alloy. For the above calculations the value of G was taken as 82 GPa, concentration (c) was taken as 6.8 wt% and ε_s was taken as 0.45.

claim: The lattice frictional stress (Peierls-Nabarro stress) τ_PN was calculated as ~154.34 MPa at 0 K, but a value of 41 MPa is used at room temperature for the Fe-6.8Al alloy, taken from literature for a 0.4 wt% C steel.
evidence_text: we obtain a value of τ_PN ~154.34 MPa. ... we have used a value of 41 MPa which has been used by Speich and Swann [46] for a 0.4 wt% C-steel.

claim: Strengthening from the ordered Fe3Al phase is described by τ_order = 0.7G (γ/Gb)^{3/2} (f r / b)^{1/2}, where γ is the antiphase boundary energy, f the fraction, and r the radius of the ordered phase.
evidence_text: The strengthening due to formation of ordered phase (τ_order) can be calculated by the equation τ_order = 0.7G (γ/Gb)^{3/2} (f r / b)^{1/2}

claim: Grain size strengthening is evaluated using the Hall-Petch relation Δσ_GB = k_HP D^{-1/2}, with the constant k_HP taken from data for 99.95% pure iron.
evidence_text: The contribution of hardening due to the presence of high angle grain boundaries (Δσ_GB) is given by the equation Δσ_GB = k_HP D^{-1/2}. ... the Hall-Petch constant for 99.95% pure iron, described in Refs. [49,50], was used.

claim: Dislocation strengthening is quantified by the Taylor equation Δσ_ρ = M α G b ρ^{1/2}, with α = 0.33, G = 82 GPa, and dislocation density ρ derived from XRD line profile analysis.
evidence_text: The strengthening contribution (Δσ_ρ) from dislocations has been considered by using the Taylor equation Δσ_ρ = M α Gb ρ^{1/2} where ρ is the dislocation density, ... α is a constant whose value is usually taken as 0.33 [50].

claim: Figure 9 presents a bar graph quantifying the contributions of solid solution, ordered phase, grain boundary, lattice friction, and dislocation strengthening to the yield strength in 98%CR, 750°C 5 min, and 750°C 60 min conditions.
evidence_text: Fig. 9 shows the contribution from each strengthening mechanism to the yield strength of Fe-6.8Al alloy in 98%CR and annealed condition (750 °C; 5 min, 60 min).

claim: In the 98% cold rolled condition, dislocation strengthening and grain boundary strengthening are the two dominant mechanisms, while after annealing at 750 °C for 60 min, ordered phase and solid solution strengthening become the major contributors.
evidence_text: For the 98%CR sample, the strengthening from (i) dislocations (generated during rolling) and (ii) grain boundaries are the two dominant mechanisms. ... For the sample annealed for 60 min, the contribution from (i) ordering and (ii) solid solution strengthening are the major contributing mechanisms.

claim: Annealing at 750 °C for 5 min produces a yield strength of 700 MPa with 20% elongation, significantly higher than the 342 MPa yield strength reported for Fe-6.8Al annealed at 900 °C for 1 min by Rana et al., which is attributed to a finer ferrite grain size (25.5±5 µm vs 69.2±34.8 µm).
evidence_text: The yield strength … annealed at 900 °C for 1 min has been described by Rana et al. [3] to be 342 MPa … lower than that obtained (YS = 700 MPa) during the annealing … at 750 °C for 5 min. This may be due to lower ferrite grain size (=25.5±5 μm, Ref. [15]) … as compared to 69.2±34.8 µm in Ref. [3].

claim: The single-phase Fe-6.8Al ferritic steel achieves a yield strength of 700 MPa and total elongation of 20% after annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, which rivals the strength of dual-phase low-density steels, mainly due to grain boundary strengthening, dislocation strengthening, and nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase.
evidence_text: the achieved strength (YS = 700 MPa, elongation = 20%) just after annealing at 750 °C for 5 min rivals to that for the dual phase low-density steels [8]. This could be due to primarily from grain boundary strengthening along with additional contribution from (i) dislocations and (ii) the formation of nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase having DO3 structure.

claim: The overall yield strength in Fe-6.8Al cold rolled and annealed samples is primarily contributed by dislocations and grain size, with additional strengthening from nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase, as evaluated by numerical formulations of known strengthening mechanisms.
evidence_text: the major contributions in the overall strength is due to dislocations and grain size. … The yield strength of the cold rolled and annealed samples were then evaluated by the numerical formulation of the known strengthening mechanisms.
